[B&B Carousell](/wiki/B%26B_Carousell "B&B Carousell")

There are also multiple other rides in Coney Island. In March 2014, construction started on the new [Thunderbolt](/wiki/Thunderbolt_(2014_roller_coaster) "Thunderbolt (2014 roller coaster)"), a [steel roller coaster](/wiki/Steel_roller_coaster "Steel roller coaster") that was manufactured by [Zamperla](/wiki/Zamperla "Zamperla") at a cost of $10 million. The ride features 2,000 feet (610 m) of track, a height of 125 feet (38 m), and a top speed of 65 miles per hour (105 km/h), as well as four [inversions](/wiki/Roller_coaster_inversions "Roller coaster inversions").[[234]](#cite_note-234) The Thunderbolt opened in June 2014.[[235]](#cite_note-235) Several [bumper car](/wiki/Bumper_car "Bumper car") rides in Coney Island are all operated separately. As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit), these include an attraction in Deno's Wonder Wheel Park,[[236]](#cite_note-denos-map-236) as well as Eldorado Auto Skooter on Surf Avenue. Historically, the earliest bumper car rides were located in Coney Island.[[237]](#cite_note-237) Furthermore, two traditional [dark ride](/wiki/Dark_ride "Dark ride") [haunted houses](/wiki/Haunted_attraction_(simulated) "Haunted attraction (simulated)") operate at Coney Island: [Spook-a-Rama](/wiki/Spook-a-Rama "Spook-a-Rama") at Deno's,[[236]](#cite_note-denos-map-236) and [Ghost Hole](/wiki/Ghost_Hole "Ghost Hole") on West 12th Street adjacent to Deno's.[[238]](#cite_note-238)

#### Former rides

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Former rides")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/ThunderboltConeyIsland1995.jpg/250px-ThunderboltConeyIsland1995.jpg)](/wiki/File:ThunderboltConeyIsland1995.jpg)

The original [Thunderbolt](/wiki/Thunderbolt_(1925_roller_coaster) "Thunderbolt (1925 roller coaster)") in 1995

Coney Island has had three major amusement parks in its past—[Steeplechase Park](/wiki/Steeplechase_Park "Steeplechase Park") (1897–1964), [Luna Park](/wiki/Luna_Park_(Coney_Island,_1903) "Luna Park (Coney Island, 1903)") (1903–1944), and [Dreamland](/wiki/Dreamland_(Coney_Island,_1904) "Dreamland (Coney Island, 1904)") (1904–1911)—as well as several standalone attractions.[[57]](#cite_note-Immerso2002-57): 74[[239]](#cite_note-239) In addition, [Astroland](/wiki/Astroland "Astroland") operated at the site of the current Luna Park from 1962 to 2008,[[240]](#cite_note-240) while a second [Dreamland](/wiki/Dreamland_(Coney_Island,_2009) "Dreamland (Coney Island, 2009)") operated at that site for only the 2009 season.[[241]](#cite_note-241)[[242]](#cite_note-242)

In addition to the rides in Coney Island's former amusement parks, there were also several dozen roller coasters that are now defunct.[[243]](#cite_note-243) The Comet, next to the Cyclone's current site, was built in 1921 and destroyed in 1945.[[3]](#cite_note-Denson_2011-3): 46 Another coaster, the Oriental Scenic Railway, was created by [LaMarcus Adna Thompson](/wiki/LaMarcus_Adna_Thompson "LaMarcus Adna Thompson") in 1887,[[57]](#cite_note-Immerso2002-57): 98–99[[3]](#cite_note-Denson_2011-3): 41 and was demolished in 1955 to be replaced with a "hot rod" amusement ride.[[101]](#cite_note-Billboard-19550129-101) The [steeplechase roller coaster](/wiki/Steeplechase_roller_coaster "Steeplechase roller coaster"), created by Steeplechase Park operator George C. Tilyou in 1897, consisted of people riding wooden horses around the park on a steel track.[[34]](#cite_note-Ierardi-34) The original wooden [Thunderbolt](/wiki/Thunderbolt_(1925_roller_coaster) "Thunderbolt (1925 roller coaster)") coaster, located between West 15th and West 16th Streets, was constructed in 1925, closed in 1983, and torn down in 2000 during the construction of nearby Keyspan Park.[[244]](#cite_note-244)[[245]](#cite_note-245) Nearby was [Tornado](/wiki/Tornado_(Coney_Island) "Tornado (Coney Island)"), a wooden coaster constructed in 1926,[[64]](#cite_note-Parascandola_2014-64): 24[[246]](#cite_note-246) and destroyed by arson in 1977.[[247]](#cite_note-247)

Coney Island also contains one defunct ride that is still standing, the [Parachute Jump](/wiki/Parachute_Jump "Parachute Jump"). Originally built as the [Life Savers](/wiki/Life_Savers "Life Savers") Parachute Jump at the [1939 New York World's Fair](/wiki/1939_New_York_World%27s_Fair "1939 New York World's Fair"), this was the first ride of its kind. Patrons were hoisted 262 ft (80 m) in the air before being allowed to drop using guy-wired parachutes. The Parachute Jump was closed in the 1960s, but was officially preserved,[[106]](#cite_note-nyt20050721-106) having been listed on the NRHP in 1980[[248]](#cite_note-248) and made a city landmark in 1989.[[249]](#cite_note-249): 1

### Beaches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Beaches")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Coney_Island_beach_%28June_2016%29.jpg/330px-Coney_Island_beach_%28June_2016%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coney_Island_beach_(June_2016).jpg)

The beach at Coney Island in June 2016, with the [Marine Parkway–Gil Hodges Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Marine_Parkway%E2%80%93Gil_Hodges_Memorial_Bridge "Marine Parkway–Gil Hodges Memorial Bridge") visible on the horizon

There is a broad public sand beach that starts at Sea Gate at West 37th Street, through the central Coney Island area and [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach "Brighton Beach"), to the beginning of the community of [Manhattan Beach](/wiki/Manhattan_Beach,_Brooklyn "Manhattan Beach, Brooklyn"), a distance of approximately 2.7 mi (4.3 km). The beach is continuous and is served for its entire length by the broad [Riegelmann Boardwalk](/wiki/Riegelmann_Boardwalk "Riegelmann Boardwalk"). Numerous amusements, as well as the aquarium and a variety of food shops and arcades, are directly accessible from the landward side of the boardwalk.[[250]](#cite_note-250)[[251]](#cite_note-La_Rocco_2004-251) The boardwalk in Manhattan Beach, located within Manhattan Beach Park, is not connected with the Riegelmann Boardwalk.[[252]](#cite_note-252)

The beaches in Coney Island used to be private until 1923 when the city bought all the land on the waterfront and created the Riegelmann Boardwalk and Beach.[[253]](#cite_note-253) Today, only the sand beach inside Sea Gate is private; it is accessible solely to residents of that community.[[251]](#cite_note-La_Rocco_2004-251)

The public beaches are maintained on a regular basis by the city. Because sand no longer naturally deposits on the beach, it is replenished in regular [beach nourishment](/wiki/Beach_nourishment "Beach nourishment") projects using [dredged](/wiki/Dredging "Dredging") sand.[[8]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-8) The public beaches are open and free to use, though the boardwalk is closed during nights from 1 to 5 a.m.[[254]](#cite_note-254) The beach area is divided into several sections by rock [groynes](/wiki/Groyne "Groyne") that were built in the 1920s to prevent erosion.[[255]](#cite_note-255): 15

There are several clubs that host activities on Coney Island's beach. The [Coney Island Polar Bear Club](/wiki/Coney_Island_Polar_Bear_Club "Coney Island Polar Bear Club") consists of a group of people who swim at Coney Island throughout the winter months. Their most popular event is an annual swim on [New Year's Day](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Day "New Year's Day").[[256]](#cite_note-256)[[3]](#cite_note-Denson_2011-3): 50 The beach also serves as the training grounds for the Coney Island Brighton Beach Open Water Swimmers, a group dedicated to promoting [open water swimming](/wiki/Open_water_swimming "Open water swimming"), which hosts several open water swim races each year.[[257]](#cite_note-257)[[258]](#cite_note-258)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Coney_island_board_walk.jpg/960px-Coney_island_board_walk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coney_island_board_walk.jpg "The boardwalk around sunset")

The boardwalk around sunset

### Public parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Public parks")]

There are several public parks in Coney Island, operated by the [New York City Department of Parks and Recreation](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Parks_and_Recreation "New York City Department of Parks and Recreation"). Parks within the main Coney Island neighborhood include:[[259]](#cite_note-259)

* The [Abe Stark](/wiki/Abe_Stark#Legacy "Abe Stark") Skating Rink, located on the south side of Surf Avenue between West 19th and West 20th Streets, adjacent to the boardwalk. It opened in 1970.[[260]](#cite_note-260)
* Coney Island Creek Park, located along the south shore of Coney Island Creek. Opened in 1984, it is composed mostly of plants.[[12]](#cite_note-NYC_Parks_CIC-12)
* Leon S. Kaiser Park, located on the northern side of Neptune Avenue between West 24th and West 32nd Streets, and contains playgrounds, athletic facilities, fitness equipment, and open spaces for barbecuing.[[11]](#cite_note-NYC_Parks_Kaiser-11)
* Poseidon Playground, located along the beach between West 25th and West 27th Streets, and contains water spray showers, playgrounds, and handball courts.[[261]](#cite_note-261)
* [Steeplechase Park](/wiki/Steeplechase_Park#Current_status "Steeplechase Park"), located along the beach between West 16th and West 19th Streets. It contains a public plaza with seating, as well as [MCU Park](/wiki/MCU_Park "MCU Park"), a [minor league baseball](/wiki/Minor_league_baseball "Minor league baseball") stadium.[[262]](#cite_note-262)
* Surf Playground, located on the south side of Surf Avenue between West 25th and West 27th Streets, just north of Poseidon Playground. It contains basketball courts, playgrounds, and water spray showers.[[263]](#cite_note-263)

### Other attractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Other attractions")]

The [New York Aquarium](/wiki/New_York_Aquarium "New York Aquarium") opened in 1957 on the former site of the Dreamland amusement park.[[102]](#cite_note-NYParks-Aquarium-History-102) It is located on 602 Surf Avenue between West 5th and West 10th Streets.[[264]](#cite_note-264) As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit), the New York Aquarium consists of five exhibits: Aquatheater; Conservation Hall; Sea Cliffs; Sharks, Rays & Turtles; and Ocean Wonders: Sharks.[[265]](#cite_note-265) The original [Bathysphere](/wiki/Bathysphere "Bathysphere"), a deep-sea submersible that made historic journeys underwater in the 1930s, is on display at the aquarium.[[266]](#cite_note-266)

Maimonides Park is located on the former site of Steeplechase Park. Opened in 2001 as KeySpan Park,[[148]](#cite_note-nyt20010626-148) it hosts the [Brooklyn Cyclones](/wiki/Brooklyn_Cyclones "Brooklyn Cyclones") minor league baseball team.[[267]](#cite_note-267) In 2010, it was renamed after the [Municipal Credit Union](/wiki/Municipal_Credit_Union "Municipal Credit Union") (MCU), the city's largest credit union, in an eleven-year naming rights deal which ended in 2021.[[268]](#cite_note-268)[[269]](#cite_note-269)

In June 2016, the [Ford Amphitheater at Coney Island](/wiki/Ford_Amphitheater_at_Coney_Island "Ford Amphitheater at Coney Island") opened on the boardwalk to the west of Maimondies Park, hosting several live musical acts as well as other events.[[270]](#cite_note-270) It was constructed at the location of the [Childs Restaurant](/wiki/Childs_Restaurants_(Coney_Island_Boardwalk_location) "Childs Restaurants (Coney Island Boardwalk location)"), which was originally constructed in 1923 and was renovated when the amphitheater was being constructed. The rooftop part of the restaurant reopened in July 2016.[[271]](#cite_note-271)

The nonprofit organization [Coney Island USA](/wiki/Coney_Island_USA "Coney Island USA") also operates the Coney Island Museum, a collection of memorabilia that chronicles the history of the neighborhood. The museum opened in 1980, and is located at 1208 Surf Avenue near the intersection with West 12th Street.[[272]](#cite_note-272) It charges a $5 admission fee per adult.[[273]](#cite_note-273)[[274]](#cite_note-274) Another nonprofit founded in 2004, the Coney Island History Project, operates a space near the Wonder Wheel.[[275]](#cite_note-275)

### Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Events")]

[![Nathan's Famous hot dog restaurant on the Coney Island boardwalk](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Nathan%27s_Coney_Island_July_2007.jpg/250px-Nathan%27s_Coney_Island_July_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nathan%27s_Coney_Island_July_2007.jpg)

[Nathan's Famous](/wiki/Nathan%27s_Famous "Nathan's Famous")

[![The Freak Bar, a Coney Island bar with a Freakshow/Sideshow theme](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Freak_bar.jpg/250px-Freak_bar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Freak_bar.jpg)

The Freak Bar

Coney Island USA sponsors various seasonal acts every year. In April, the organization hosts the Noisefest and the Congress of Curious Peoples. This is followed in May or June by the [Coney Island Mermaid Parade](/wiki/Coney_Island_Mermaid_Parade "Coney Island Mermaid Parade"), which takes place on Surf Avenue and the boardwalk, and features [floats](/wiki/Float_(parade) "Float (parade)") and performances. During August or September, Coney Island USA produces the Beard and Moustache Competition; Tattoo and Motorcycle Festival; and Coney Island Film Festival. The organization then hosts the Creepshow at the Freakshow, an interactive [Halloween](/wiki/Halloween "Halloween")-themed event, in October.[[276]](#cite_note-276)

The annual Cosme 5K Charity Run/Walk, supported by the Coney Island Sports Foundation, takes place on the Riegelmann Boardwalk toward the end of June.[[277]](#cite_note-277)

A major national [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball") tournament hosted by the [Association of Volleyball Professionals](/wiki/Association_of_Volleyball_Professionals "Association of Volleyball Professionals") (AVP), which is typically hosted on the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") of the U.S., was held in Coney Island starting in 2006. The AVP built a 4,000-seat stadium and twelve outer courts next to the boardwalk for the event.[[278]](#cite_note-278)[[279]](#cite_note-279) When AVP tournaments resumed in Brooklyn in 2015, they were hosted at [Brooklyn Bridge Park](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge_Park "Brooklyn Bridge Park") instead.[[280]](#cite_note-280)

In 2009, the [Ringling Bros. and Barnum & Bailey Circus](/wiki/Ringling_Bros._and_Barnum_%26_Bailey_Circus "Ringling Bros. and Barnum & Bailey Circus") performed in Coney Island for the first time since 1956. The event, titled *The Coney Island Boom-A-Ring*, was housed in tents that were located between the boardwalk and Surf Avenue.[[281]](#cite_note-bp20100604-281) The following year, they returned to the same location with *The Coney Island Illuscination*.[[281]](#cite_note-bp20100604-281)

In May 2015, Thor Equities unveiled Coney Art Walls, a public art wall project curated by former [Museum of Contemporary Art, Los Angeles](/wiki/Museum_of_Contemporary_Art,_Los_Angeles "Museum of Contemporary Art, Los Angeles"), director [Jeffrey Deitch](/wiki/Jeffrey_Deitch "Jeffrey Deitch") and Thor CEO Joe Sitt. Located at 3050 Stillwell Avenue, the project featured work from more than 30 artists.[[282]](#cite_note-282) The exhibition started being held annually through at least 2019.[[283]](#cite_note-283)

From 2001 to 2010, *[The Village Voice](/wiki/The_Village_Voice "The Village Voice")* held the annual [Siren Music Festival](/wiki/Siren_Music_Festival "Siren Music Festival") on Coney Island. The event featured mostly [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") and [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") acts like [M.I.A.](/wiki/M.I.A._(rapper) "M.I.A. (rapper)"), [Beach House](/wiki/Beach_House "Beach House"), [TV on the Radio](/wiki/TV_on_the_Radio "TV on the Radio"), and [Modest Mouse](/wiki/Modest_Mouse "Modest Mouse").[[284]](#cite_note-284)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/W15_CI_volunteers_arrive_Sandy_jeh.jpg/250px-W15_CI_volunteers_arrive_Sandy_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:W15_CI_volunteers_arrive_Sandy_jeh.jpg)

Volunteers arrive to clear the boardwalk after [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy").

Based on data from the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"), the combined population of Coney Island and Sea Gate was 31,965, a decrease of 2,302 (6.7%) from the 34,267 counted in [2000](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"). Covering an area of 851.49 acres (344.59 ha), the neighborhood had a population density of 37.5 inhabitants per acre (24,000/sq mi; 9,300/km2).[[285]](#cite_note-285)

The racial makeup of the neighborhood was 32.2% (10,307) [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 30.9% (9,880) [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 8.7% (2,793) [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.2% (78) [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 0.0% (4) [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 0.2% (67) from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 1.5% (467) from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race were 26.2% (8,369) of the population.[[286]](#cite_note-PLP3A-286) 82% of the population were [high school](/wiki/High_school "High school") graduates and 40% had a [bachelor's degree](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree") or higher.[[286]](#cite_note-PLP3A-286)[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 2

The entirety of Community Board 13 had 106,459 inhabitants as of [NYC Health](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Health_and_Mental_Hygiene "New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene")'s 2018 Community Health Profile, with an average life expectancy of 80.4 years.[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 2, 20 This is lower than the median life expectancy of 81.2 for all New York City neighborhoods.[[288]](#cite_note-:21-288): 53 (PDF p. 84) Most inhabitants are adults, with 25% between the ages of 25–44, 27% between 45 and 64, and 22% who are at least 65 years old. The ratio of young and college-aged residents was lower, at 19% and 8%, respectively.[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 2 Coney Island's elderly population, as a share of the area's total population, is higher than in other New York City neighborhoods.[[289]](#cite_note-:14-289): 6

As of 2016, the median [household income](/wiki/Household_income "Household income") in Community District 13 was $39,213.[[290]](#cite_note-290) In 2018, an estimated 24% of Coney Island residents lived in poverty, compared to 21% in all of Brooklyn and 20% in all of New York City. One in eight residents (11%) were unemployed, compared to 9% in the rest of both Brooklyn and New York City. Rent burden, or the percentage of residents who have difficulty paying their rent, is 55% in Coney Island, slightly higher than the citywide and boroughwide rates of 52% and 51%, respectively. Based on this calculation, as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit), Coney Island is not considered to be [gentrifying](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification").[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 7

According to the 2020 census data from [New York City Department of City Planning](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_City_Planning "New York City Department of City Planning"), there were between 20,000 and 29,999 White residents, 10,000 to 19,999 Black residents, 5,000 to 9,999 Hispanic residents, and less than 5000 Asian residents.[[291]](#cite_note-291)[[292]](#cite_note-292)

## Political representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Political representation")]

Politically, Coney Island is in [New York's 8th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_8th_congressional_district "New York's 8th congressional district").[[293]](#cite_note-293)[[294]](#cite_note-294) It is also in the [New York State Senate](/wiki/New_York_State_Senate "New York State Senate")'s 23rd district,[[295]](#cite_note-295)[[296]](#cite_note-296) the [New York State Assembly](/wiki/New_York_State_Assembly "New York State Assembly")'s 46th district,[[297]](#cite_note-297)[[298]](#cite_note-298) and the [New York City Council](/wiki/New_York_City_Council "New York City Council")'s 47th district.[[299]](#cite_note-299)

## Police and crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Police and crime")]

Coney Island is patrolled by the [New York City Police Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department") (NYPD)'s 60th Precinct, located at 2950 West Eighth Street.[[4]](#cite_note-NYPD_60th_Precinct-4) Transit District 34 is located at 1243 Surf Avenue, within the [Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_(New_York_City_Subway) "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue (New York City Subway)") subway station.[[300]](#cite_note-300)

The 60th Precinct ranked 34th safest out of 69 patrol areas for per-capita crime in 2010. Between 1993 and 2010, major crimes decreased by 72%, including a 76% decrease in robberies, 71% decrease in felony assaults, and 67% decrease in shootings.[[301]](#cite_note-301) As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit), with a non-fatal assault rate of 51 per 100,000 people, Coney Island's rate of [violent crimes](/wiki/Violent_crime "Violent crime") per capita is less than that of the city as a whole. The incarceration rate of 168 per 100,000 people is about the same as that of the city as a whole.[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 8 The 60th Precinct has a substantially lower crime rate than in the 1990s, with crimes across all categories having decreased by 77.5% between 1990 and 2022. The precinct reported five murders, 16 rapes, 179 robberies, 373 felony assaults, 159 burglaries, 527 grand larcenies, and 121 grand larcenies auto in 2022.[[302]](#cite_note-302)

## Fire safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Fire safety")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/CI_Engine_318_jeh.jpg/250px-CI_Engine_318_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:CI_Engine_318_jeh.jpg)

Engine Co. 318/Ladder Co. 166

The [New York City Fire Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Fire_Department "New York City Fire Department") (FDNY) operates two [firehouses](/wiki/Firehouse "Firehouse") in the area.[[5]](#cite_note-fdny-locs-5) Engine Company 318/Ladder Company 166 is located at 2510 Neptune Avenue.[[303]](#cite_note-303) It contains the [Coney Island Fire Station Pumping Station](/wiki/Coney_Island_Fire_Station_Pumping_Station "Coney Island Fire Station Pumping Station"), [listed](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Brooklyn "National Register of Historic Places listings in Brooklyn") on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[304]](#cite_note-304) Engine Company 245/Ladder Company 161/Battalion 43 is located at 2929 West 8th Street.[[305]](#cite_note-305) In addition, [FDNY EMS](/wiki/New_York_City_Fire_Department_Bureau_of_EMS "New York City Fire Department Bureau of EMS") Station 43 is on the grounds of Coney Island Hospital.[[306]](#cite_note-306)

## Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Health")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/CIH_%40_Shore_%26_Ocean_Pkwys_jeh.jpg/250px-CIH_%40_Shore_%26_Ocean_Pkwys_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:CIH_@_Shore_%26_Ocean_Pkwys_jeh.jpg)

Coney Island Hospital

As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit), [preterm births](/wiki/Preterm_birth "Preterm birth") and births to teenage mothers are slightly more common in Coney Island than in other places citywide. In Coney Island, there were 95 preterm births per 1,000 live births (compared to 87 per 1,000 citywide) and 20.2 births to teenage mothers per 1,000 live births (compared to 19.3 per 1,000 citywide), slightly higher than in the median neighborhood.[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 11 Coney Island has a high population of residents who are [uninsured](/wiki/Health_insurance_coverage_in_the_United_States "Health insurance coverage in the United States"), or who receive healthcare through [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid").[[289]](#cite_note-:14-289) In 2018, this population of uninsured residents was estimated to be 14%, which is higher than the citywide rate of 12%.[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 14

The concentration of [fine particulate matter](/wiki/Particulates "Particulates"), the deadliest type of [air pollutant](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution"), in Coney Island is 0.0067 milligrams per cubic metre (6.7×10−9 oz/ft3), lower than the citywide and boroughwide averages.[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 9 Nineteen percent of Coney Island residents are [smokers](/wiki/Smoking "Smoking"), which is higher the city average of 14% of residents being smokers.[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 13 In Coney Island, 28% of residents are [obese](/wiki/Obesity "Obesity"), 15% are [diabetic](/wiki/Diabetes_mellitus "Diabetes mellitus"), and 31% have [high blood pressure](/wiki/Hypertension "Hypertension")—higher than the citywide averages of 24%, 11%, and 28%, respectively.[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 16 In addition, 18% of children are obese, compared to the citywide average of 20%.[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 12

Ninety-two percent of residents eat some fruits and vegetables every day, which is slightly higher than the city's average of 87%. In 2018, 70% of residents described their health as "good", "very good", or "excellent", lower than the city's average of 78%.[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 13 For every supermarket in Coney Island, there are 21 [bodegas](/wiki/Convenience_store "Convenience store").[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 10 The primary hospital in the neighborhood is [Coney Island Hospital](/wiki/Coney_Island_Hospital "Coney Island Hospital").[[289]](#cite_note-:14-289): 6 In addition, [NYC Health + Hospitals](/wiki/NYC_Health_%2B_Hospitals "NYC Health + Hospitals")' Ida G. Israel Community Health Center is on Surf Avenue, and there is a substance-abuse treatment center on West 16th Street.[[307]](#cite_note-307)[[308]](#cite_note-308)

## Post offices and ZIP Codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Post offices and ZIP Codes")]

Coney Island's primary [ZIP Code](/wiki/ZIP_Code "ZIP Code") is 11224,[[309]](#cite_note-309) though small portions located east of West 1st Street and [Ocean Parkway](/wiki/Ocean_Parkway_(Brooklyn) "Ocean Parkway (Brooklyn)") are located in ZIP Code 11235.[[310]](#cite_note-310) There are two [United States Post Office](/wiki/United_States_Post_Office "United States Post Office") branches in Coney Island. The Coney Island Station is located at 2727 Mermaid Avenue,[[311]](#cite_note-311) and the Neptune Station is located at 532 Neptune Avenue.[[312]](#cite_note-312)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Education")]

Coney Island generally has a similar ratio of college-educated residents to the rest of the city as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit). While 45% of residents age 25 and older have a college education or higher, 18% have less than a high school education and 37% are high school graduates or have some college education. By contrast, 40% of Brooklynites and 38% of city residents have a college education or higher.[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 6 The percentage of Coney Island students excelling in math has been increasing, though reading achievement has declined; math achievement rose from 53 percent in 2000 to 72 percent in 2011, but reading achievement fell from 57 to 55 percent within the same time period.[[313]](#cite_note-313)

Coney Island's rate of elementary school student absenteeism is higher than the rest of New York City. In Coney Island, 26% of elementary school students missed twenty or more days per [school year](/wiki/School_year "School year"), compared to the citywide average of 20% of students.[[288]](#cite_note-:21-288): 24 (PDF p. 55)[[287]](#cite_note-CHP2018-287): 7

### Elementary, middle, and high schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Elementary, middle, and high schools")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Abraham_Lincoln_High_School.jpg/250px-Abraham_Lincoln_High_School.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abraham_Lincoln_High_School.jpg)

Abraham Lincoln High School

Coney Island is served by the [New York City Department of Education](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Education "New York City Department of Education"), and students in the neighborhood are automatically "zoned" into the nearest public schools. The zoned schools for the main portion of Coney Island include:

* PS 90 Edna Cohen School (grades K-5)[[314]](#cite_note-314)[[315]](#cite_note-315)
* PS 100 Coney Island School (grades K-5)[[316]](#cite_note-316)[[317]](#cite_note-nyt20061119-317)
* PS 188 The Michael E. Berdy School (grades K-4)[[318]](#cite_note-318)
* PS/IS 288 The Shirley Tanyhill School (grades PK-8)[[319]](#cite_note-319)
* IS 303 Herbert S. Eisenberg (grades 6–8)[[317]](#cite_note-nyt20061119-317)[[320]](#cite_note-320)[[321]](#cite_note-321)
* PS 329 (grades PK-5)[[322]](#cite_note-322)

IS 239, the [Mark Twain School for the Gifted and Talented](/wiki/Mark_Twain_School_for_the_Gifted_and_Talented "Mark Twain School for the Gifted and Talented") (6–8), is a [magnet school](/wiki/Magnet_school "Magnet school") for gifted students, and it accepts students from around the city.[[323]](#cite_note-323) In 2006, David Scharfenberg of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* said, "Coney Island's elementary schools are a mixed lot, with only some exceeding citywide averages on the state's testing regimen."[[317]](#cite_note-nyt20061119-317)

All New York City high school students can go to any high school in the city. There are two public high schools in Coney Island: [Abraham Lincoln High School](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln_High_School_(Brooklyn) "Abraham Lincoln High School (Brooklyn)")[[317]](#cite_note-nyt20061119-317)[[324]](#cite_note-324) and [Rachel Carson High School for Coastal Studies](/wiki/Rachel_Carson_High_School_for_Coastal_Studies "Rachel Carson High School for Coastal Studies").[[325]](#cite_note-325)

### Public library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Public library")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/CI_BPL_1901_Mermaid_Av_jeh.jpg/250px-CI_BPL_1901_Mermaid_Av_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:CI_BPL_1901_Mermaid_Av_jeh.jpg)

Coney Island library branch

The [Brooklyn Public Library](/wiki/Brooklyn_Public_Library "Brooklyn Public Library") (BPL)'s Coney Island branch is located at 1901 Mermaid Avenue, near the intersection with West 19th Street. It opened in 1911 as an unmanned deposit station. Ten years later, it moved to the former *[Coney Island Times](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island_Times&action=edit&redlink=1 "Coney Island Times (page does not exist)")* offices and became fully staffed. In 1954 another branch was built. According to BPL's website, the library was referred to as "the first-ever library built on stilts over the Atlantic Ocean." The branch was rebuilt in 2013 after being damaged in Hurricane Sandy.[[326]](#cite_note-326)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Coney_IslandStillwell.JPG/250px-Coney_IslandStillwell.JPG)](/wiki/File:Coney_IslandStillwell.JPG)

[Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue station](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_station "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue station")

Coney Island is served by four [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") stations.[[327]](#cite_note-327)[[328]](#cite_note-MTA-NameofNeighborhood-2015-328) The [Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue station](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_station "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue station"), the terminal of the [D](/wiki/D_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "D (New York City Subway service)")​, [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)"), [<F>](/wiki/Fd_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Fd (New York City Subway service)")​​, [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)")​, and [Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)") trains, is one of the largest elevated rapid transit stations in the world, with eight tracks serving four platforms.[[329]](#cite_note-MTA-Stillwell-2014-329) The entire station, built in 1917–1920 as a replacement for the former surface-level [Culver Depot](/wiki/Culver_Depot "Culver Depot"),[[330]](#cite_note-330) was rebuilt in 2001–2004.[[149]](#cite_note-nyt20050528-149)[[329]](#cite_note-MTA-Stillwell-2014-329) The other subway stations within Coney Island are [West Eighth Street–New York Aquarium](/wiki/West_Eighth_Street%E2%80%93New_York_Aquarium_station "West Eighth Street–New York Aquarium station"), served by the F, <F>​​, and Q trains; [Neptune Avenue](/wiki/Neptune_Avenue_station "Neptune Avenue station"), served by the F and <F>​ trains; and [Ocean Parkway](/wiki/Ocean_Parkway_station "Ocean Parkway station"), served by the Q train.[[328]](#cite_note-MTA-NameofNeighborhood-2015-328)

A [bus terminal](/wiki/Bus_station "Bus station") beneath the Stillwell Avenue station serves the [B68](/wiki/B68_(New_York_City_bus) "B68 (New York City bus)") to [Prospect Park](/wiki/Prospect_Park_(Brooklyn) "Prospect Park (Brooklyn)"), the [B74](/wiki/B74_(New_York_City_bus) "B74 (New York City bus)") to Sea Gate, the [B64](/wiki/B64_(New_York_City_bus) "B64 (New York City bus)") to [Bay Ridge](/wiki/Bay_Ridge,_Brooklyn "Bay Ridge, Brooklyn"), and the [B82](/wiki/B82_(New_York_City_bus) "B82 (New York City bus)") to [Canarsie](/wiki/Canarsie,_Brooklyn "Canarsie, Brooklyn") and [Starrett City](/wiki/Starrett_City,_Brooklyn "Starrett City, Brooklyn"). Additionally, the [B36](/wiki/B36_(New_York_City_bus) "B36 (New York City bus)") runs from Sea Gate to [Sheepshead Bay](/wiki/Sheepshead_Bay,_Brooklyn "Sheepshead Bay, Brooklyn"). The [B1](/wiki/B1_(New_York_City_bus) "B1 (New York City bus)") and [B4](/wiki/B4_(New_York_City_bus) "B4 (New York City bus)") both make stops on the neighborhood's border with [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach,_Brooklyn "Brighton Beach, Brooklyn") along [Ocean Parkway](/wiki/Ocean_Parkway_(Brooklyn) "Ocean Parkway (Brooklyn)"), between Shore Parkway, Neptune and Brighton Beach Avenues. The [X28](/wiki/X28_(New_York_City_bus) "X28 (New York City bus)") and [X38](/wiki/X38_(New_York_City_bus) "X38 (New York City bus)") provide express bus service to Manhattan.[[331]](#cite_note-331)

The three main west–east arteries in the neighborhood are (from north to south) Neptune Avenue, Mermaid Avenue, and Surf Avenue. Neptune Avenue becomes Emmons Avenue at Sheepshead Bay, while Surf Avenue becomes [Ocean Parkway](/wiki/Ocean_Parkway_(Brooklyn) "Ocean Parkway (Brooklyn)") and then runs north toward Prospect Park. The north–south cross streets in Coney Island are numbered, with "West" prepended to their numbers. The street numbers run from West 1st Street at Coney Island's eastern border to West 37th Street at the western border, adjacent to Sea Gate.[[332]](#cite_note-332)

Coney Island contains several [bicycle paths](/wiki/Cycling_in_New_York_City "Cycling in New York City"). The Ocean Parkway bicycle path terminates in the neighborhood, while the [Shore Parkway](/wiki/Shore_Parkway "Shore Parkway") bike path (part of the [Brooklyn Waterfront Greenway](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Waterfront_Greenway&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brooklyn Waterfront Greenway (page does not exist)")) runs east along [Jamaica Bay](/wiki/Jamaica_Bay "Jamaica Bay") and west and north along New York Harbor. On-street bike lanes are marked on Neptune Avenue and other streets in Coney Island. In addition, the Riegelmann Boardwalk is open to cyclists during the daytime, though bicycling hours are restricted during the summer months.[[333]](#cite_note-333)

In 2019, [NYC Ferry](/wiki/NYC_Ferry "NYC Ferry") announced that the western part of Coney Island would be served by the Coney Island ferry route.[[334]](#cite_note-334)[[335]](#cite_note-335)[[336]](#cite_note-336) The implementation of the Coney Island route was delayed indefinitely in 2022.[[337]](#cite_note-The_City_2022-337)[[338]](#cite_note-338)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

Main article: [Coney Island in popular culture](/wiki/Coney_Island_in_popular_culture "Coney Island in popular culture")

Coney Island has been featured in many novels, films, television shows, cartoons, and theatrical plays.[[57]](#cite_note-Immerso2002-57): 176[[339]](#cite_note-Rabinovitz_2004-339) This is linked to its iconic status as a vacation destination.[[340]](#cite_note-340) Various [slapstick](/wiki/Slapstick "Slapstick") comedies and films have been set at Coney Island or allude to it. There have also been several television documentaries about the area's history.[[339]](#cite_note-Rabinovitz_2004-339): 137–142[[341]](#cite_note-341)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coney_Island&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Notable people")]

Notable current and former residents of Coney Island include:

See also: [Category:People from Coney Island](/wiki/Category:People_from_Coney_Island "Category:People from Coney Island")

* [Bud Abbott](/wiki/Bud_Abbott "Bud Abbott") (1897–1974), comedian, actor and producer. He was best known as the straight man half of the comedy duo [Abbott and Costello](/wiki/Abbott_and_Costello "Abbott and Costello")[[342]](#cite_note-342)
* [Ken Auletta](/wiki/Ken_Auletta "Ken Auletta") (born 1942), author, political columnist for the *[New York Daily News](/wiki/New_York_Daily_News "New York Daily News")* and media critic for *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")*[[343]](#cite_note-343)
* [Joe Bonomo (strongman)](/wiki/Joe_Bonomo_(strongman) "Joe Bonomo (strongman)") (1901–1978), weightlifter, strongman, film stunt performer and actor[[344]](#cite_note-344)
* [Jerry Della Femina](/wiki/Jerry_Della_Femina "Jerry Della Femina") (born 1936), advertising executive and restaurateur[[345]](#cite_note-345)
* [Mary E. Dillon](/wiki/Mary_E._Dillon "Mary E. Dillon") (1886–1983), businesswoman and president of Brooklyn Borough Gas Company[[346]](#cite_note-346)
* [Jeffrey Epstein](/wiki/Jeffrey_Epstein "Jeffrey Epstein") (1953–2019), financier and child sex offender[[347]](#cite_note-347)
* [Harold Feinstein](/wiki/Harold_Feinstein "Harold Feinstein") (1931–2015), photographer[[348]](#cite_note-348)
* [Gene Feist](/wiki/Gene_Feist "Gene Feist") (1923–2014), playwright, theater director and co-founder of the [Roundabout Theater Company](/wiki/Roundabout_Theater_Company "Roundabout Theater Company")[[349]](#cite_note-349)
* [Irving Feldman](/wiki/Irving_Feldman "Irving Feldman") (born 1928), poet and professor of English[[350]](#cite_note-350)
* [Sandra Feldman](/wiki/Sandra_Feldman "Sandra Feldman") (1939–2005), educator and labor leader who served as president of the [American Federation of Teachers](/wiki/American_Federation_of_Teachers "American Federation of Teachers")[[351]](#cite_note-351)
* [Nat Finkelstein](/wiki/Nat_Finkelstein "Nat Finkelstein") (1933–2009), photographer and photojournalist[[352]](#cite_note-352)
* [Debbie Goad](/wiki/Debbie_Goad "Debbie Goad") (1954–2000), journalist and assistant editor of the magazine *[Answer Me!](/wiki/Answer_Me! "Answer Me!")*[[353]](#cite_note-353)
* [Arlene Gottfried](/wiki/Arlene_Gottfried "Arlene Gottfried") (1950–2017), street photographer who was known for recording the candid scenes of ordinary daily life[[354]](#cite_note-354)
* [Gilbert Gottfried](/wiki/Gilbert_Gottfried "Gilbert Gottfried") (1955–2022), stand-up comedian and actor, best known for his exaggerated shrill voice[[355]](#cite_note-355)
* [Marty Greenbaum](/wiki/Marty_Greenbaum "Marty Greenbaum") (1934–2020), painter, mixed media assemblage and book artist[[356]](#cite_note-356)
* [Pamela Harris](/wiki/Pamela_Harris_(politician) "Pamela Harris (politician)"), politician who was a member of the [New York State Assembly](/wiki/New_York_State_Assembly "New York State Assembly") representing the 46th Assembly District from 2015 to 2018[[357]](#cite_note-357)
* [Marcus Illions](/wiki/Marcus_Illions "Marcus Illions") (c. 1871–1949), master carver of wooden carousel horses in the early 20th century, who was called "the Michelangelo of carousel carvers" by *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*[[358]](#cite_note-358)
* [Rena Kanokogi](/wiki/Rena_Kanokogi "Rena Kanokogi") (1935–2009), judo expert[[359]](#cite_note-359)
* [Robert Kirsch](/wiki/Robert_Kirsch "Robert Kirsch") (1922–1980), literary critic and author, who was the literary editor of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* for two decades[[360]](#cite_note-360)
* [Stephon Marbury](/wiki/Stephon_Marbury "Stephon Marbury") (born 1977), former professional basketball player[[361]](#cite_note-361)
* [Pellegrino Morano](/wiki/Pellegrino_Morano "Pellegrino Morano") (1877–unknown), head of a group of Neapolitan criminals with roots in the [Camorra](/wiki/Camorra "Camorra")[[362]](#cite_note-362)
* [Nikita Nesterenko](/wiki/Nikita_Nesterenko "Nikita Nesterenko") (born 2001), professional ice hockey center who currently plays for the [San Diego Gulls](/wiki/San_Diego_Gulls "San Diego Gulls")[[363]](#cite_note-363)
* [Rhea Perlman](/wiki/Rhea_Perlman "Rhea Perlman") (born 1948), actress[[364]](#cite_note-364)
* [E. J. Perry](/wiki/E._J._Perry_(artist) "E. J. Perry (artist)") (1880–1946), early-twentieth-century silhouette artist[[365]](#cite_note-365)
* [Joe Rollino](/wiki/Joe_Rollino "Joe Rollino") (1905–2010), weightlifter and strongman who dubbed himself the world's strongest man in the 1920s[[366]](#cite_note-366)
* [Larry Rosenberg](/wiki/Larry_Rosenberg "Larry Rosenberg") (born 1932), Buddhist teacher[[367]](#cite_note-367)
* [Jay Sexter](/wiki/Jay_Sexter "Jay Sexter"), educator who was the president of [Mercy College](/wiki/Mercy_University "Mercy University")[[368]](#cite_note-368)
* [Ayisha Siddiqa](/wiki/Ayisha_Siddiqa "Ayisha Siddiqa") (born 1999), climate justice advocate[[369]](#cite_note-369)
* [Don Snyder](/wiki/Don_Snyder "Don Snyder") (1934–2010), photographer and multimedia artist[[370]](#cite_note-370)
* [George C. Tilyou](/wiki/George_C._Tilyou "George C. Tilyou") (1862–1914), entrepreneur and showman who founded [Steeplechase Park](/wiki/Steeplechase_Park "Steeplechase Park")[[371]](#cite_note-371)
* [Burt Topper](/wiki/Burt_Topper "Burt Topper") (1928–2007), film director and screenwriter best known for cult films[[372]](#cite_note-372)
* [Arthur Tress](/wiki/Arthur_Tress "Arthur Tress") (born 1940), photographer known for his staged surrealism[[373]](#cite_note-373)
